sections.heading: Service Accounts Overview
sections.body:
Service accounts are the recommended method to manage authentication to
the Atlas Administration API. Service accounts provide improved security over
API (Application Programming Interface) keys by using the industry standard [OAuth 2.0](https://oauth.net/2/) protocol with the
[Client Credentials flow](https://www.oauth.com/oauth2-servers/access-tokens/client-credentials/).

A service account lets you manage permissions and create access tokens
that authenticate API (Application Programming Interface) requests. Each service account has a client ID
and a secret that function as a username and a password for creating
access tokens. To learn how to construct an API (Application Programming Interface) request with an access
token, see [Make an API (Application Programming Interface) Request](example-api-request).

> **Note:** [IP Access List](access-list) apply to *using* service account access tokens, not
> [cloud.mongodb.com/api/oauth/token](generate-oauth2-token-atlas) or [revoking](revoke-oauth2-token-atlas) them.
> You can generate a token from any IP address, but you can only use it to call
> the API (Application Programming Interface) if your IP address is on the access list.

Access tokens are only valid for 1 hour (3600 seconds) per the
[OAuth 2.0](https://oauth.net/2/) specification. This limited lifespan protects against replay
attacks, where an attacker uses a leaked access token without time
restriction.

Each service account belongs to exactly one organization, and you can
grant it access to any number of projects within that organization. To
give an organization-level service account access to a project, see
[Grant Existing Service Account Access to a Project](invite-org-app-api-keys).

Atlas [Atlas User Roles](user-roles) limit which operations a service
account can authenticate with its access token. You must assign roles to
service accounts as you would for users to ensure that the service
account generates access tokens with the necessary permissions for the
desired API (Application Programming Interface) calls.

You can't use a service account or its access token to log into
Atlas through the Atlas UI. Service accounts only grant access
to the Atlas Administration API, which does not include UI access or access
to cluster data. To learn more about the limitations of service
accounts, see [MongoDB Limits and Thresholds](manual/reference/limits).

sections.heading: API Keys Overview
sections.body:
> **Important:** We recommend you use service accounts instead of API (Application Programming Interface) keys to
> authenticate to the Atlas Administration API. API (Application Programming Interface) keys are a legacy
> authentication method.

API keys are a legacy method of authenticating to the
Atlas Administration API that uses [HTTP Digest Authentication](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Digest_access_authentication).

API (Application Programming Interface) keys have two parts: a Public Key and a Private Key. These serve
the same function as a username and a password to authenticate API (Application Programming Interface)
requests. To learn how to construct an API (Application Programming Interface) request using API (Application Programming Interface) keys,
see [Make an API (Application Programming Interface) Request](example-api-request).

Atlas hashes the Public Key and Private Key using a unique value
called a **nonce**. The nonce is only valid for a short amount of time
as per the [HTTP Digest Authentication](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Digest_access_authentication) specification. This limited lifespan protects
against replay attacks, where an attacker caches a Private Key to use it
without time restriction.

Each pair of API (Application Programming Interface) keys belongs to only one organization, and can grant
access to any number of projects in that organization. To give
organization-level API (Application Programming Interface) keys access to a project, see
[Grant Existing Service Account Access to a Project](invite-org-app-api-keys).

Atlas [Atlas User Roles](user-roles) limit which operations API (Application Programming Interface) keys
can perform. You must assign roles to API (Application Programming Interface) keys as you would for users
to ensure the API (Application Programming Interface) keys have the necessary permissions for the desired
API (Application Programming Interface) calls.

You can't use API (Application Programming Interface) keys to log into Atlas through the Atlas UI.
API (Application Programming Interface) keys only grant access to the Atlas Administration API, which does not
include UI access or access to cluster data.

sections.heading: Next Steps
sections.body:
To learn how to use and manage service accounts and API (Application Programming Interface) keys, see the
following procedures:

- [create a new one](create-org-api-key)

- [Programmatic API Keys](about-project-api-keys)

- [Manage Programmatic Access to an Organization](atlas-admin-api-access-org)

- [Manage Programmatic Access to a Project](atlas-admin-api-access-project)

- [Manage Programmatic Access to Multiple Organizations](prog-access-mult-orgs)

- [cloud.mongodb.com/api/oauth/token](generate-oauth2-token-atlas)

- [revoking](revoke-oauth2-token-atlas)

> **See Also:** [Configure Digest Authentication for Node, Python, and Ruby](https://www.mongodb.com/developer/products/atlas/nodejs-python-ruby-atlas-api/)
